learning_outcomes.description: Identify an appropriate methodology of comparative law.
Understand the fundamental principles underlying constitutional and federal theories.
Distinguish constitutions according to the appropriate typologies.
Compare and evaluate different constitutions worldwide.

assessments.task_description:
Students are required to complete an online test. The quiz consists of multiple-choice and/or true or false questions. The test will assess material covered in the seminars from weeks 2 to 8. This test is designed to assess the comprehension of the basic principles and concepts in comparative constitutional law, constitutional theory, and the US Constitution. This will ensure students understand the content covered to date and are satisfactorily following the learning materials.

The test will open at 8 am Tuesday, 22 September, and close at 8 pm Wednesday, 23 September.

The quiz will contain 20 questions. Each question is worth 1 mark. Incorrect answers are awarded no marks.

The quiz will be time-limited to 30 minutes. Once started, the quiz must be completed in one sitting. Do not leave the test before clicking Save and Submit.

There will be no opportunities for resits. Before sitting the test, please ensure that you have sufficient time and appropriate working conditions to complete it.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.due_date: The topics will vary each week, and allocations will be made in the first seminar (Week 1) of the course.
assessments.task_description:
Students are required to deliver a short presentation on a specific feature or dimension of comparative constitutional law and constitutional theory. Topics will be set out on the Blackboard site with the dates on which they are to be presented. The topics will vary each week, and allocations will be made in the first seminar (Week 1) of the course.

To present well, you should conduct comparative research and adopt a theoretical or analytical perspective on the history, development, or emerging issue related to the theme of your presentation and the country/constitution you have chosen. The presentation must be related to the constitution of a country other than Australia (except when indicated otherwise). You may discuss your topic choice with the course coordinator.

There will be deductions (1 mark per 15 seconds) for exceeding the stipulated time limit. After each presentation, there is an opportunity for questions from fellow students and the lecturer. Responses to these questions will form part of the overall mark. This is to ensure authenticity and the level of knowledge and understanding of the topic. Presentations will typically be held in the final hour of the seminar.

Each presentation must be accompanied by one e-poster (eg, one PowerPoint slide or PDF/JPEG image) and a list of references (bibliography). Students are required to submit their e-posters and bibliography through the course coordinator's email at least 12 hours before their presentation.

  • The e-poster must be made with still images (that is, it must not contain any animations or motions).
  • Text should be kept to a minimum in the poster. There should be no more than 50 words in the e-poster.
  • References in the Bibliography should follow the AGLC4 style and rules.

Students are marked according to their oral presentation, response to questions, bibliography/legal research, and e-poster.

Students should be aware that, according to UQ policy, the presentations will be recorded.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Each presentation must be accompanied by one e-poster (PDF, JPEG or PowerPoint slide) and a .doc (Word) file containing a reference list (with an AI use disclosure).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
This is a time-limited essay (in-person final exam).

The exam consists of at least two questions, but students can choose which question to answer. Students need to answer only one question. There are no word limits for the final exam.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Definitions & Methodology

Introduction to the field of Comparative Constitutional Law: Is comparative law a proper field of law? What are the methodologies we can use to compare constitutions? What are some common terms and definitions we find in comparative constitutional law?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Definitions & Methodology Introduction to the field of Comparative Constitutional Law: Is comparative law a proper field of law? What are the methodologies we can use to compare constitutions? What are some common terms and definitions we find in comparative constitutional law?

learning_activities.topic:
Constitutionalism: Ancient and Modern

Where does the idea of constitutionalism come from? Is constitutionalism an ancient or modern development? How has the idea of constitutionalism evolved? What are the main principles of constitutionalism?

Learning outcomes: L02, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Constitutionalism: Ancient and Modern Where does the idea of constitutionalism come from? Is constitutionalism an ancient or modern development? How has the idea of constitutionalism evolved? What are the main principles of constitutionalism?

learning_activities.topic:
Constitutional Typologies

How are different constitutions categorised? What are the distinct typologies and classifications of constitutions?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Constitutional Typologies How are different constitutions categorised? What are the distinct typologies and classifications of constitutions?

learning_activities.topic:
Constitutional Formation & Constituent Power

How are constitutions created? What is constituent power? Who has the legitimacy to enact a constitution?

Learning outcomes: L02, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Constitutional Formation & Constituent Power How are constitutions created? What is constituent power? Who has the legitimacy to enact a constitution?

learning_activities.topic:
Constitutional Amendment & Judicial Review

How are constitutions changed? Who can change a constitution? What processes are involved in materially or formally changing a constitution? What is the role of constitutional courts? How do constitutional courts operate?

Learning outcomes: L02, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Constitutional Amendment & Judicial Review How are constitutions changed? Who can change a constitution? What processes are involved in materially or formally changing a constitution? What is the role of constitutional courts? How do constitutional courts operate?

learning_activities.topic:
Federalism & Federations

What is federalism? What is a federation? What are examples of federal countries? How are they similar, and how do they differ?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Federalism & Federations What is federalism? What is a federation? What are examples of federal countries? How are they similar, and how do they differ?

learning_activities.topic:
The US Constitution: History and Key Features

Why is the US Constitution so famous? How was it created? What are the key aspects of the US Constitution? What are some of its landmark constitutional cases?

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_title: The US Constitution: History and Key Features
learning_activities.topic_description: The US Constitution: History and Key Features Why is the US Constitution so famous? How was it created? What are the key aspects of the US Constitution? What are some of its landmark constitutional cases?

learning_activities.topic:
'Global North' Constitutions

What are the key features of some of the 'Global North' Constitutions?

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: 'Global North' Constitutions What are the key features of some of the 'Global North' Constitutions?

learning_activities.topic:
The Australian Constitution in Context

What can we learn about the Australian Constitution when looking abroad? What principles and provisions have we inherited from abroad? What is distinctive about our Constitution?

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: The Australian Constitution in Context What can we learn about the Australian Constitution when looking abroad? What principles and provisions have we inherited from abroad? What is distinctive about our Constitution?
